pdg_node.statement: (csgraph: sparse matrix (N×N), directed: bool, indices: int or array-like of int, return_predecessors: bool, unweighted: bool, limit: float, min_only: bool) -> tuple[ndarray shape (K,N) or (N,), ndarray shape (K,N) or (N,)]
pdg_node.informal_desc: Computes shortest-path distances from one or more source nodes to all reachable nodes. Dijkstra's greedy algorithm is used for non-negative weights; Bellman-Ford's edge-relaxation handles negative weights. Both share the same intent — locate optimal routes from a set of origins — and return a distance matrix plus an optional predecessor map.

declaration.name: singlesourceshortestpath
declaration.type_signature: (csgraph: sparse matrix (N×N), directed: bool, indices: int or array-like of int, return_predecessors: bool, unweighted: bool, limit: float, min_only: bool) -> tuple[ndarray shape (K,N) or (N,), ndarray shape (K,N) or (N,)]
declaration.docstring: Computes shortest-path distances from one or more source nodes to all reachable nodes. Dijkstra's greedy algorithm is used for non-negative weights; Bellman-Ford's edge-relaxation handles negative weights. Both share the same intent — locate optimal routes from a set of origins — and return a distance matrix plus an optional predecessor map.
declaration.conceptual_summary: MultiOriginOptimalCostPropagation. Given a weighted relational structure encoded as a sparse N×N adjacency matrix, this operation propagates cumulative traversal costs outward from one or more designated origin nodes, resolving the globally optimal (minimum-cost) scalar accumulation path to every reachable node in the structure. Two relaxation strategies are supported: a greedy priority-queue expansion (valid when all edge weights are non-negative) and an iterative edge-relaxation sweep (valid for arbitrary real-valued weights including negative), both converging to the same fixed-point solution — a cost-distance field over the node set. An optional predecessor map is co-emitted, encoding the reverse-traceable arborescence of optimal paths rooted at each origin.. Applications: Computational neuroscience — mapping minimum-synaptic-hop or minimum-conduction-delay paths across a connectome modeled as a weighted directed graph, identifying structurally critical relay nodes between cortical regions, Supply-chain & logistics optimization — computing least-cost routing from K distribution centers to all N demand nodes in a capacitated transport network, where edge weights encode fuel cost, toll fees, or transit time, Epidemiology & diffusion modeling — measuring the minimum weighted-contact-chain distance from a set of index cases to all susceptible individuals in a contact network, informing containment radius and priority vaccination scheduling, Integrated circuit & VLSI design — determining minimum-latency signal propagation paths from clock sources or I/O pins to all internal logic gates in a timing graph, where edge weights represent gate delays or wire capacitances

pdg_node.statement: (csgraph: sparse matrix (N×N), directed: bool, return_predecessors: bool, unweighted: bool) -> tuple[ndarray (N,N), ndarray (N,N)]
pdg_node.informal_desc: Computes the full N×N shortest-path distance matrix for every source–destination pair simultaneously using the Floyd-Warshall dynamic-programming recurrence. Suitable for dense reachability queries where all pairwise distances are required at once.

declaration.name: allpairsshortestpath
declaration.type_signature: (csgraph: sparse matrix (N×N), directed: bool, return_predecessors: bool, unweighted: bool) -> tuple[ndarray (N,N), ndarray (N,N)]
declaration.docstring: Computes the full N×N shortest-path distance matrix for every source–destination pair simultaneously using the Floyd-Warshall dynamic-programming recurrence. Suitable for dense reachability queries where all pairwise distances are required at once.
declaration.conceptual_summary: GlobalPairwiseClosureMatrix. Applies a dynamic-programming recurrence over an N×N weighted adjacency structure to simultaneously resolve the minimum-cost transitive path between every ordered pair of nodes, yielding a fully dense closure matrix. At each relaxation step, an intermediate pivot node is considered as a potential relay, and pairwise distances are updated if routing through that pivot reduces total accumulated cost. The result is the metric closure of the input relation — a complete, consistent distance space where the triangle inequality is globally enforced.. Applications: Computational neuroscience — constructing a full pairwise functional-connectivity distance matrix across all recorded neural units in a connectome, enabling cluster detection and information-flow bottleneck analysis without repeated single-source traversals, Supply-chain logistics — computing the globally optimal inter-facility transfer-cost table across all warehouse–depot pairs in a distribution network, used as a precomputed lookup for real-time routing decisions under dynamic demand, Computational linguistics — deriving a complete semantic-distance matrix between every pair of nodes in a lexical knowledge graph (e.g., a thesaurus or ontology), enabling similarity queries and analogical reasoning by direct lookup rather than on-demand traversal, Epidemiological modeling — resolving the minimum-step contagion-pathway matrix between all pairs of geographic or demographic nodes in a contact network, supporting rapid identification of shortest transmission chains and critical bridge nodes for intervention targeting

declaration.name: allpairsshortestpath

pdg_node.statement: (csgraph: sparse matrix (N×N), overwrite: bool) -> CSR sparse matrix (N×N)
pdg_node.informal_desc: Extracts the globally optimal minimum spanning tree of a sparse undirected weighted graph. Returns a new sparse matrix whose non-zero entries correspond only to MST edges, minimizing total edge weight while keeping the graph connected.

declaration.name: minimumspanningtree
declaration.type_signature: (csgraph: sparse matrix (N×N), overwrite: bool) -> CSR sparse matrix (N×N)
declaration.docstring: Extracts the globally optimal minimum spanning tree of a sparse undirected weighted graph. Returns a new sparse matrix whose non-zero entries correspond only to MST edges, minimizing total edge weight while keeping the graph connected.
declaration.conceptual_summary: GloballyOptimalAcyclicConnectivityExtractor. Given a symmetric, non-negatively weighted adjacency structure over N discrete nodes, this operation selects the minimal-weight subset of exactly N-1 edges that preserves full connectivity, eliminating all redundant paths and cycles while minimizing the aggregate scalar cost of the retained topology. The result is a lossless-connectivity, lossy-structure projection that collapses a potentially dense relational graph into its irreducible acyclic backbone — a spanning tree — whose total edge weight is globally minimized across all possible spanning trees of the input.. Applications: Computational Neuroscience — Extracting the minimal-weight axonal wiring backbone from a full pairwise functional-connectivity matrix of cortical regions, reducing a dense N×N correlation tensor to the irreducible acyclic scaffold that preserves whole-brain reachability at lowest total metabolic signaling cost, Supply Chain & Logistics Engineering — Given a weighted adjacency matrix of pairwise transport costs between N distribution nodes (warehouses, cities, relay stations), computing the minimum-cost acyclic routing skeleton that keeps all nodes reachable, serving as the seed topology for further capacity-optimization or flow-routing layers, Genomics & Phylogenetics — Constructing a minimum-cost evolutionary tree from an N×N pairwise genetic-distance matrix over N taxa or protein sequences, yielding an acyclic hierarchical topology that spans all observed sequences with the least total mutational distance — the parsimony backbone prior to clade refinement, Sensor Network Design & Embedded Systems — Given an N×N matrix of pairwise RF link quality or energy-cost estimates among N sensor nodes, identifying the minimum total-energy spanning communication topology that keeps the entire network connected, used as the baseline mesh prior to fault-tolerance redundancy augmentation
